items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: What does inheritance allow a subclass to do?
items.correct: Reuse and extend the attributes and methods of its superclass.
items.random_wrong: Copy code from any unrelated class.
items.why: Students think inheritance overrides everything. It actually REUSES the parent's members (which can be added to or overridden), creating an 'is-a' relationship.

items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.q: Hoare's quicksort algorithm was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1959. “1945” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Binary search time complexity (on sorted array):
items.why: The answer examiners want is: O(log n). “O(1)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 1959 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
